text: This is a robotic right hemicolectomy for a unresectable cecal polyp.
The surgeon observes the tattooed area of the cecal polyp during the robotic right hemicolectomy.
The first step in the operation is restoration of normal anatomy.
The surgeon uses robotic scissors to sharply dissect and remove scar tissue along the lateral abdominal wall from a previous appendectomy.
The surgeon begins medial to lateral mobilization of the colon by incising the peritoneum below the ileocolic pedicle using robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to create a window beneath the ileocolic pedicle, carefully dissecting into the tissue plane to prepare for vessel division.
Thankfully, this patient is quite skinny, and it made it much easier.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide the ileocolic pedicle, which is visible beneath the opened peritoneum.
I feel it does a very nice job.
The surgeon continues dissection in the avascular plane, gently sweeping the duodenum and pancreas inferiorly.
The surgeon navigates through the avascular plane, aiming for a mostly bloodless operation while approaching the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon completes the takedown of the hepatic flexure, visibly separating the tissue planes in this region.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide vessels along the lateral wall, maintaining precision in the avascular plane.
The surgeon continues to operate within the avascular plane, performing a bloodless division of vessels that traverse from the retroperitoneum to the colon.
The surgeon divides the colon mesentery, progressing towards the transverse colon, while maintaining a bloodless field in the avascular plane.
The surgeon prepares to perform the division at the hepatic flexure, focusing on maintaining a bloodless field by staying within the correct avascular plane.
The surgeon takes down the omentum located on the anti-mesenteric surface, carefully separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies a division point on the transverse colon, preparing for a future anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to divide the colon, with the administration of indocyanine green (ICG) by the anesthesiologist to enhance visualization of the tissue perfusion.
The surgeon places a surgical stapler across the transverse colon, visually assessing the perfusion of the colon tissue to ensure proper placement.
Blood supply is paramount in any colon surgery and using ICG is a way to preserve and verify that you have adequate blood flow to the remaining colon.
The surgeon begins dividing the mesentery of the ileum, preparing the proximal transection point by carefully separating the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon carefully assesses the mesentery of the ileum to ensure maintained blood flow while preparing for lymph node harvest.
The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments of the colon, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon inspects the dissection field to ensure no retroperitoneal structures, such as the ureter or kidney, have inadvertently entered the area.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to sweep through the surgical field, ensuring they are in the proper anatomical plane during the dissection.
The surgeon dissects and removes the film attachments along the lateral abdominal wall and the white line of Toldt, ensuring clear visibility through the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to perform the ileal division after the colon has been completely mobilized.
The surgeon administers another dose of ICG, causing the ileum to fluoresce green, indicating adequate perfusion. The ileum is then divided using the robotic stapler.
The surgeon passes the divided specimen above the liver for safekeeping, ensuring it is securely positioned away from the surgical field.
The surgical team prepares for the iliocolonic anastomosis, positioning instruments and aligning the ileum and colon for connection.
The surgeon positions the transverse colon and removes sections of the omentum to create a clear space for the colotomy and the upcoming iliocolonic anastomosis.
The surgeon positions the ileum in opposition to the transverse colon, preparing for the anastomosis between these two sections.
I think that looks really nicely.
The surgeon places a stay stitch at the apex of the surgical site, providing stability for the fourth iron during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a stay stitch to control tension during the iliocolonic anastomosis, ensuring precise alignment between the ileum and transverse colon.
The surgeon creates colotomies by aligning the two limbs of the intestine against each other, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates an opening in the ileum and aligns it with the transverse colon before making a similar opening in the colon.
The surgeon uses a combination of cutting and coagulation techniques to access the colon and small bowel, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon positions a surgical stapler to perform the anastomosis between the ileum and the colon, aligning the two sections for stapling.
The surgeon adjusts the positioning of the ileum and colon for the anastomosis, encountering minor bleeding, which is controlled using a bipolar cautery device.
The surgeon maneuvers the two limbs of the ileum and colon against each other, aligning them for the anastomosis.
The surgeon fires the stapler to complete the anastomosis between the ileum and colon, securing the connection between the two structures.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close the common channel, securing the anastomosis between the ilium and colon.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture, threading the end through the tissue, which self-locks to secure the closure.
The surgeon begins closing the anastomosis at the bottom corner, ensuring precise alignment of the tissue edges.
The surgeon meticulously over-sews the corner of the anastomosis, ensuring a secure closure to prevent potential leaks.
The surgeon secures the bottom corner of the anastomosis and begins suturing mucosa to mucosa, using a technique that ensures precise alignment of the tissue layers.
The surgeon reaches the superior apex of the anastomosis and begins a lumbar suture, stitching from the top down to the bottom of the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a two-layer closure of the common enterotomy using a 2-0 V-Loc suture, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue layers.
Here I'm more worried about getting the tissue in opposition than I am narrowing.
The surgeon uses limber sutures to cover the common channel of a large and widely patent anastomosis, ensuring complete coverage.
The surgeon assesses the blood supply to the area, ensuring it appears adequate, while placing sutures across the common channel to achieve complete closure.
The surgeon visually inspects the anastomosis site, checking for patency and ensuring the integrity of the closure.
It looks good.
The surgeon positions a segment of omentum over the anastomosis to serve as a buttress, ensuring it covers the area effectively.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and retrieves the specimen through a twelve millimeter port.
And with that concludes the case.
Thank you for watching.
